sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Bag valve mask (BVM) ventilation delivers positive-pressure breaths to patients who are apnoeic or have inadequate respiratory effort. It is a fundamental airway skill used in resuscitation and pre-intubation management. Effective BVM ventilation requires a patent airway, a good mask seal, and appropriate tidal volume.

sections.title: Equipment
sections.content:
- BVM device (adult: 1500 mL bag; paediatric: 500 mL bag)
- Appropriate mask size (fits bridge of nose to chin cleft)
- Oxygen supply and tubing
- Oropharyngeal (OPA) or nasopharyngeal (NPA) airway adjunct
- Suction available

sections.title: Procedure
sections.content:
**Single-rescuer technique:**

1. Position patient supine; open airway with head-tilt chin-lift (or jaw thrust if spinal precautions)

2. Insert OPA/NPA if tolerated

3. Select correct mask size

4. Apply mask using C-E grip: thumb and index finger form 'C' over mask; remaining three fingers form 'E' under mandible to lift jaw

5. Maintain seal while squeezing bag with other hand

6. Deliver breath over 1 second — enough to produce visible chest rise

7. Rate: 10–12/min adult (1 breath every 5–6 seconds); 12–20/min paediatric

**Two-rescuer technique (preferred):**

1. Rescuer 1: applies mask with both hands using two-thumb technique for superior seal

2. Rescuer 2: squeezes bag

**Tidal volume:** ~500–600 mL adult (approximately half to two-thirds bag squeeze)

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Avoid over-ventilation — excessive tidal volume causes gastric insufflation and aspiration risk
- Attach oxygen at 15 L/min with reservoir bag for FiO₂ ~0.85
- If chest does not rise: reposition head, reassess seal, check for obstruction, suction
- Consider 2-person technique whenever possible — significantly improves tidal volume delivery
- During CPR: 30:2 ratio (unsynchronised) or 1 breath per 6 seconds with continuous compressions (advanced airway in place)
